screens.name: Music font style lab
screens.purpose: Compare SMuFL engraving fonts against the same symbol-rich guitar score.

screens.name: Song guitar score
screens.purpose: Let a guitarist read chord symbols and six-string tablature and follow the score directly in the browser.

ux_rules: Keep one dominant play/pause action; restart and tempo are subordinate.
Place transport controls immediately before the score they operate.
Treat chord names, notation, and TAB as aligned layers of one score rather than separate cards.
Render a clear chords-only message when tablature events are absent.
Use elapsed-time playback so pausing and resuming remain stable.
Avoid repeating a system label when the section heading already names the local context.
Reduce measures per row at narrow widths instead of forcing page-level horizontal scrolling.

components: responsive combined chord and TAB system

screen_contexts.layout_intent: A dense but calm sheet-music canvas with compact controls and stable left alignment across headings, chord symbols, strings, and barlines.
interaction_risks: TAB numbers collide at narrow widths.
Transport chrome competes with the score.
Chord-only songs appear broken rather than intentionally supported.
notes: Current page has no page-level horizontal overflow at 1440px.
Current renderer provides chord staff rows but no guitar TAB or playback controls.

constraints: Keep the engraving-font and chord-font selectors in one compact control strip above the score.
Use Finale Maestro as the initial engraving font and provide Edwin, JazzCord, and Campania as independently switchable chord-name fonts.
Use one identical alphaTex demo for every font so visual comparisons remain valid.
Destroy and recreate AlphaTabApi when changing SMuFL fonts because alphaTab cannot swap music fonts dynamically.
Show loading and actionable error states while font files and metadata are fetched.
Fit the rendered score to the pane at desktop and narrow widths without hiding representative notation systems.
open_decisions: Gootville represents the SMuFL-compatible Gonville lineage.
MScore represents the SMuFL-compatible MuseScore/Emmentaler lineage.

constraints: Use Scheherazade New Bold at 19px as the fixed production chord font and remove the comparison selector.
Left-align every chord name at its beat or bar anchor so long chord names never extend left of a measure boundary.
Keep four measures in every system at phone widths, scaling the engraving to the available pane without horizontal overflow.
Use the global header gutter system for the entire song page: 24px on phones, 40px from medium widths, and 64px from large widths.
Do not add page-specific maximum widths, nested title gutters, or alphaTab horizontal page padding inside the global gutter.
Hide measure numbers on every system.
Position chord names slightly lower than the previous Edwin treatment while preserving a visible gap above the staff.
Keep at least one clear text-line of vertical separation between the initial tempo marking and first chord name; effect labels must never touch or visually merge.
Justify the final score system to the full available width even when it contains fewer measures; every system must share the same left and right boundaries.
Production song detail pages contain only the page title, artist, and score; do not repeat song metadata inside the score or add renderer, font, tempo, or chart header chrome.
Never render track names, staff names, instrument labels, or other content to the left of a score system; every system must reserve the same left origin so measure columns align across rows.
Use Finale Maestro engraving and Edwin chord names as the fixed production typography for Songs.
Keep font experimentation in the Score Lab and remove the font selector from production Songs chord charts.
Preserve the App Router server boundary and isolate playback state in the smallest practical client component.
Use existing theme tokens, Geist typography, and lucide icons; do not add a component library.
Keep the score background warm and quiet, with functional accent reserved for playback state.
Use responsive row chunking so the working pane fits before considering horizontal overflow.
Use semantic button elements with visible focus and accessible names.
Make string labels and fret values real accessible text where practical, while decorative lines remain hidden.
Keep proprietary Guitar Pro import/export explicitly outside the implementation.
open_decisions: Audio synthesis remains out of scope.
Editing and file import remain future capabilities.

critical_judgments: Finale Maestro and Edwin are selected on first load.
Edwin, JazzCord, and Campania visibly change chord-name lettering without changing the engraving font.
Each option visibly changes clefs, noteheads, rests, accidentals, flags, and repeat glyphs.
The same demo contains standard notation, TAB, chords, accidentals, rests, articulations, ties or slides, and repeat structures.
The selected font name and compatibility note are visible without duplicating labels.
Desktop and narrow layouts fit the available pane.
must_not_regress: Existing Songs chord charts use the fixed Scheherazade New chord treatment without a local font selector.
The font lab does not depend on copyrighted song notation.

critical_judgments: The combined system reads unmistakably as guitar tablature.
The play/pause action is visually dominant but does not overpower the score.
Chord, fret, barline, and cursor alignment stays legible across representative rows.
Desktop and narrow layouts avoid page-level overflow and clipped supporting chrome.
Rendered section and system labels are not redundant.
must_not_regress: Song navigation and metadata remain available.
Existing repeat, ending, time-signature, and jump marks remain legible.
No cold techno chrome, bright accent blocks, or card fragmentation.

visual_constraints: Use warm paper-like surfaces and dark brown-black notation ink from existing theme tokens.
Keep controls in one compact horizontal region with restrained borders and radius.
Render six evenly spaced TAB strings, left-side string labels, measure barlines, and fret numbers with mono typography.
Render the cursor as a thin muted-accent line spanning the active score layers.
At narrow widths, show fewer measures per row rather than shrinking fret numbers below a readable size.
